metrics.narrative: The buyer articulated clear operational pain but has not quantified financial impact. Chris Burris (Purchasing Manager) stated on 22 Apr 2026: 'We are spending a lot of time on administrative and paperwork duties that are a little bit of a time waste when we could be using that time for more strategic procurement items' and 'The team spends excessive time manually sending emails to suppliers with purchase orders, while not prioritizing efforts to seek competitive quotes or explore new suppliers.' Two specific pain points surfaced: (1) manual PO emailing to suppliers consuming purchasing team time, and (2) lack of systematic quote comparison leading to repeat ordering from the same suppliers without competitive evaluation. The buyer also noted maintenance/engineering staff place orders with single quotes that purchasing cannot review due to bandwidth constraints. However, no quantified savings target, headcount impact, cycle-time reduction, or ROI expectation was established. The rep presented a $70-110k annual cost estimate and mentioned Zycus builds business-case decks with projected 5-year savings from prior case studies, but no customer-specific value case was co-developed on the call. Gap: quantify the time/cost waste from manual PO distribution and lost savings from non-competitive ordering, and build a mutual ROI model anchored to their Toyota/automotive operational tempo. This is a required step before EB/CFO approval.

champion.narrative: Chris Burris (Purchasing Manager, cburris@cmwa.com) is a developing champion but not yet a true advocate with power or access. On the 22 Apr 2026 introductory call, Chris engaged positively, asked clarifying questions, and explicitly reoriented the conversation to prioritize eSourcing and eProcurement (against the rep's initial assumption that contract/risk management would be the focus), showing genuine interest and willingness to guide the evaluation to his team's real needs. He asked about pricing, next steps, and timeline, and agreed to receive demo videos and follow-up in 2 weeks. However, he explicitly stated he is conducting research 'on my own so far' with 'no internal discussions' yet, meaning he has zero executive sponsorship, no access to the Economic Buyer or decision-making authority, and no ability to drive internal approval. He acknowledged that securing buy-in from 'the big wigs or the higher ups' is required but has not initiated that process. His engagement was warm but passive — he did not volunteer to introduce other stakeholders, schedule a follow-up meeting, or take any internal action on our behalf. Since the 22 Apr call, there has been no contact for 36 days (65 days as of today 26 Jun), and the planned 2-week follow-up is 7+ weeks overdue with no evidence Chris has re-engaged or asked for the demo/next steps. This suggests his interest may be cooling or he is waiting for us to re-initiate, but he is not actively championing Zycus internally. He is single-threaded (zero other buyer contacts on the opp) and has no demonstrated advocacy, access to power, or urgency. Status: developing, but at risk of fading. Next move: re-engage Chris, deliver the promised resources and demo, and ask him to introduce the budget owner and bring in IT/operations stakeholders for a discovery call to multi-thread and test his willingness to advocate.

competition.narrative: Four competitors logged in Salesforce (Coupa, GEP Worldwide, JAGGAER, SAP Ariba), and the buyer confirmed on 22 Apr 2026 that Zycus was the second vendor contacted, meaning he is running a parallel multi-vendor evaluation. No competitor was named explicitly on the call, but the Salesforce Competitor field is treated as accurate given the buyer's stated early research across multiple vendors. The competitive positioning on the call was strong: the rep led with AI differentiation (AI embedded since 2016, Merlin negotiation agents, AI-powered vendor search and RFP grading), demonstrated the full Source-to-Pay wheel with organic integration vs. bolted-together competitor suites, and emphasized ease of use (Microsoft Teams integration, low learning curve for small teams). Chris responded positively to the AI/automation story, especially the autonomous negotiation agents and quote comparison capabilities, which directly address his pain. However, no explicit competitive wedge or counter-positioning against Coupa/GEP/JAGGAER/Ariba was delivered, and no follow-up competitive differentiation materials (e.g., Gartner report, customer references vs. competitors, AI capability comparison) were sent. The risk: with 36 days of silence and no demo delivered, competitors may have advanced their evaluations while we stalled. The buyer is in early research with no internal discussions yet, meaning the competitive shortlist is still fluid — we could be eliminated passively if we don't re-engage and differentiate. Wedge: Zycus's AI-native automation (Merlin negotiation, RFP grading, vendor search) vs. competitors' bolt-on AI, and ease of use for small procurement teams (Microsoft Teams integration, low training burden) vs. complex enterprise suites. Activation: deliver a demo focused on eSourcing/eProcurement with live AI workflows (quote comparison automation, Merlin negotiation agent), share the Gartner Magic Quadrant report positioning Zycus as Leader/Visionary vs. competitors, and provide a small-procurement-team customer reference (automotive/manufacturing preferred) showing fast time-to-value and cost savings vs. Coupa or Ariba.

identify_pain.narrative: Two primary operational pain points confirmed, both articulated by Chris Burris (Purchasing Manager) on 22 Apr 2026. Pain #1: Excessive time spent on manual, low-value administrative tasks. Chris stated: 'We are spending a lot of time on administrative and paperwork duties that are a little bit of a time waste when we could be using that time for more strategic procurement items' and 'The team spends excessive time manually sending emails to suppliers with purchase orders, while not prioritizing efforts to seek competitive quotes or explore new suppliers.' This pain is owned by Chris and his small purchasing team, who are underwater with repetitive PO distribution and unable to focus on strategic sourcing. Pain #2: Lack of systematic competitive quoting and supplier evaluation. Chris explained: 'a lot of times, we will either just keep ordering from the same supplier over and over without really doing a quote comparison on a regular basis' and 'one of our guys on the floor, like maintenance or from engineering, maybe place an order and they will just maybe get one quote, and because our department is not huge, we're not able to spend the time analyzing that, and we really just order.' This results in (inferred) lost cost savings, Maverick spend from non-procurement stakeholders, and repeat ordering from incumbents without competitive pressure. Chris explicitly prioritized eSourcing and eProcurement modules to address these pains over contract/risk management. Additional operational pain mentioned: responding to customer supply-chain-risk surveys (e.g., conflict minerals, weather/geopolitical disruptions) by surveying suppliers without verification, creating compliance risk. However, Chris deprioritized supplier risk management in favor of quote automation. The pain is clear and owned; the gap is quantification (how much time, how much lost savings, how many non-competitive orders per month) to build a compelling business case for budget approval.

ai_fit_signal.summary: The buyer's AI appetite is pragmatic/functional rather than strategic. Chris Burris expressed strong interest in automation to reduce manual tasks (PO emailing, quote comparison) and responded positively to the AI capabilities demoed (Merlin negotiation agents, AI RFP grading, vendor search), especially the quote-comparison and negotiation-agent workflows. However, he framed his interest in operational efficiency ('reduce time waste on admin tasks, focus on strategic procurement'), not AI-native transformation or innovation. There was no mention of a broader AI strategy, executive mandate, or digital transformation initiative at Central Motor Wheel. The AIS (AI excitement) fields in Salesforce are null, and the buyer is in early vendor research with no internal discussions yet, suggesting AI is a means to an end (efficiency/cost savings), not a strategic driver. This is a solid AI Curious signal — the buyer sees value in AI-powered automation and is open to Zycus's AI differentiation, but AI itself is not the compelling event or budget unlock. The wedge is to position Merlin/AI as the competitive advantage that delivers the operational outcomes (faster quoting, lower cost, less manual work) he is measured on, not AI for AI's sake.

Chris Burris explicitly prioritized eSourcing and eProcurement modules for these pain points, and the rep demonstrated AI-powered quote comparison, vendor search, RFP grading, and Merlin autonomous negotiation agents — all of which address the buyer's stated gaps. Chris responded positively to the AI/automation positioning and the ease-of-use story (Microsoft Teams integration, low learning curve). However, one potential exposure: Plex ERP integration.
